--

In the previous article, I learned about Python data types and how different types of data are stored in a program. Understanding data types is important, but knowing how to work with those data types is just as essential.
In this article, I’ll explore Python operators and type conversion. Operators allow us to perform calculations, compare values, and evaluate conditions, while type conversion helps us change one data type into another when needed.
While practicing Python, I found that operators and type conversion are used almost everywhere. Whether you’re doing simple calculations or preparing data for analysis, these concepts help Python process data correctly.
As always, this article is part of my learning journey, and I hope it can also help other beginners understand these concepts more easily.

1. What is an Operator?
In Python, an operator is a symbol used to perform an action on one or more values. Depending on the operator, it can be used to calculate numbers, compare values, or evaluate logical conditions.

• Perform mathematical calculations

• Compare two values

• Evaluate logical conditions
Without operators, Python would not be able to perform calculations or make decisions based on data.
2. Arithmetic Operators
Arithmetic operators are used to perform mathematical calculations.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

table 1Example
a = 10
b = 3
print(a + b)
print(a - b)
print(a * b)
print(a / b)
print(a % b)
print(a ** b)
print(a // b)Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 1You’ll use arithmetic operators whenever you need to perform calculations in Python. They are commonly used to calculate totals, averages, percentages, or other numerical values, especially when working with datasets.
3. Comparison Operators
Comparison operators compare two values and return either True or False.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

table 2Example
print(10 > 5)
print(10 == 10)
print(8 != 3)Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 2Comparison operators are useful when checking whether certain conditions are met.
4. Logical Operators
Logical operators are used to combine multiple conditions. Python provides three main logical operators:

• and

• or

• not
Example
age = 20
print(age > 18 and age < 30)Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 3Another example:
print(age < 18 or age > 30)Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 4Logical operators become especially useful when working with conditional statements, which I’ll cover in the next article.

5. What is Type Conversion?
Sometimes, data is not stored in the format we need. For example, numbers entered by users are often stored as strings. Before performing calculations, we need to convert them into numeric data types. This process is called type conversion.
6. Common Type Conversions
Integer to Float
print(float(10))Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 5Float to Integer
print(int(3.75)) Output
3Notice that Python removes the decimal part instead of rounding the number.
String to Integer
print(int("20"))Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 6List to String
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
print(str(numbers))Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 7Boolean to Integer
print(int(True))
print(int(False))Output:
1
0Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 8Python treats True as 1 and False as 0.
7. A Common Beginner Mistake
One common mistake is trying to combine different data types without converting them.
For example:
print("5" + 5)Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 9Python raises an error because it cannot concatenate a string with an integer. The correct way is to convert the string into an integer first.
print(int("5") + 5)Output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 10Understanding this concept can help avoid many common beginner errors.
8. Operations on Different Data Types
String Operations
Strings can be concatenated using the + operator.
print("Hello " + "Anggrey")Strings can also be repeated.
print("Hi " * 3)
Dictionary Operations
Dictionary values are accessed using their keys.
student = {
"name": "Anggrey",
"age": 20
}print(student["name"])
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image 12While learning Python, I realized that understanding data types alone isn’t enough. We also need to know how to manipulate and work with those data types using operators and type conversion.
Although these concepts seem simple, they are used constantly when writing Python programs and preparing data for analysis. Learning them has helped me better understand how Python processes information behind the scenes.
In the next part of this series, I’ll learn about conditional statements (if, elif, and else) and explore how Python makes decisions based on different conditions.
